attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with persistent abnormal beliefs. She has a medical history of polysubstance use and multiple episodes of drug-induced psychosis. Her most recent episode of drug-induced psychosis was six months ago, and she reports being substance-free since that time. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears anxious but cooperative. Her vital signs are within normal limits. During the interview, she reports hearing voices internally. Her thought process is disorganised, and she expresses the fixed belief that she is trapped in an alternate universe. She insists that her sister, mother, and father are not who they appear to be, stating that her sister who visits her is actually an alien pretending to be her sister.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the patient's abnormal belief?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's delusional belief that her family members, particularly her sister, have been replaced by imposters (in this case, an alien) represents Capgras syndrome. This syndrome is characterised by the fixed false belief that a person, typically someone familiar or emotionally significant to the patient, has been replaced by an identical-looking imposter. It is often seen in paranoid schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, and affective disorders, but can also occur in neurodegenerative conditions and after brain injury.

Nihilistic delusion refers to the belief that oneself, others, or the world does not exist or has been destroyed. This type of delusion often involves beliefs about catastrophic events or the end of the world. The patient in this case is not expressing nihilistic beliefs but rather misidentification delusions about her family members.

Somatic delusional disorder involves the persistent belief that one has a medical issue, such as a parasitic infection, despite evidence to the contrary. The patient in this scenario is not expressing concerns about her physical health but rather about the identity of her family members.

Erotomania, also known as De Clerambault syndrome, is characterised by the delusional belief that one is being loved by another person, often of higher social status. It can occur alongside paranoia vera, paranoid schizophrenia, or other forms of paranoid disorder. The patient is not expressing beliefs about being loved by someone else.

Othello syndrome is characterised by the delusional belief that one's romantic partner is being unfaithful. This morbid jealousy can lead to repeated accusations, attempts to monitor the partner's behaviour, and sometimes violence. The patient in this case is not expressing jealousy or concerns about infidelity but rather misidentification delusions about her family members.

The patient's presentation with internal auditory hallucinations, thought disorder, and the specific delusion that her family members have been replaced by imposters most closely aligns with Capgras syndrome, which is often associated with psychotic disorders including schizophrenia and schizoaffective disorder.
learningPoints: Capgras syndrome is the delusional belief that a familiar person has been replaced by an identical-looking imposter.
Capgras syndrome can occur in various psychiatric conditions including schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, and affective disorders.
Delusional disorders are characterised by fixed false beliefs that persist despite evidence to the contrary.
references: Scott S. Capgras' Syndrome and the Delusions of Misidentification. Psychiatric Annals [Internet]. 1992 ;22(5):279-285. Available from: https://doi.org/10.3928/0048-5713-19920501-13

Jordan HW, Howe G. De Clerambault syndrome (erotomania): a review and case presentation. J Natl Med Assoc. 1980 Oct;72(10):979-85. PMID: 6999163; PMCID: PMC2552541. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2552541/

Kataoka H, Sugie K. Delusional Jealousy (Othello Syndrome) in 67 Patients with Parkinson’s Disease. Frontiers in Neurology [Internet]. 2018;9. Available from: https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fneur.2018.00129/full#:~:text=are%20visual%20hallucinations.-,Othello%20syndrome%20(OS)%20is%20a%20type%20of%20paranoid%20delusional%20jealousy,on%20unfounded%20evidence%20(4).
Mad, bad and paranoid - and persistent too [Internet]. the Guardian. 2005. Available from: https://www.theguardian.com/money/2005/nov/13/observercashsection.theobserver1
Delusional Disorder: Causes, Symptoms, Types & Treatment [Internet]. Cleveland Clinic. 2022. Available from: https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/9599-delusional-disorder
